course_info.title: Network Security
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Design a defense-in-depth network architecture with multiple security layers
Implement network segmentation strategies using VLANs and subnets
Apply zero trust network principles to modern enterprise environments
Evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of perimeter-based vs zero trust models
readings: Stallings, Network Security Essentials, Ch. 1-2
NIST SP 800-207: Zero Trust Architecture
Palo Alto Networks, Network Segmentation Best Practices whitepaper

lecture_notes.introduction: Network security architecture defines how an organization protects its digital assets at the network layer. For decades, the dominant model was perimeter-based defense: a firewall at the network edge separating the trusted internal network from the untrusted internet, with a DMZ for public-facing services. This model assumed that internal traffic was trustworthy, an assumption shattered by modern threats. Lateral movement after initial compromise, insider threats, and cloud migration have eroded the relevance of the perimeter. Zero trust architecture eliminates implicit trust based on network location, requiring every access request to be authenticated, authorized, and encrypted regardless of whether the source is inside or outside the traditional perimeter. Defense-in-depth layers multiple independent security controls so that the failure of any single control does not compromise the system. A well-designed network security architecture combines segmentation (limiting blast radius), monitoring (detecting threats), access control (enforcing policy), and encryption (protecting data in transit). This course takes you from architectural principles through hands-on implementation of every major network security technology.
key_concepts: Defense-in-depth: layered security controls where each layer independently provides protection -- network, host, application, data
Network segmentation: dividing the network into isolated zones using VLANs, subnets, and firewalls to limit lateral movement
DMZ (Demilitarized Zone): a network segment between the internet and internal network hosting public-facing services
Zero Trust Architecture: no implicit trust based on network location; every access request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted
Micro-segmentation: applying firewall policies at the individual workload level using software-defined networking (SDN)
Security zones: Internet, DMZ, Internal, Management, Guest -- each with distinct trust levels and inter-zone rules
East-west traffic: lateral traffic within the network (server-to-server) versus north-south traffic (client-to-server across the perimeter)
Network security assessment methodology: asset inventory, threat modeling, gap analysis, control implementation, continuous monitoring
Least privilege networking: default-deny firewall rules where only explicitly permitted traffic flows between zones
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a segmented network for a small company with pfSense firewalls: Internet-facing DMZ with web server and email relay, internal corporate zone with workstations and file server, management zone with admin jump box, and isolated guest Wi-Fi. Configure VLANs on a virtual switch, apply zone-based firewall rules with default deny, and verify that guest traffic cannot reach the corporate zone while DMZ servers can reach only their required internal services.
setup_requirements: pfSense VM or GNS3 with firewall and switch images
Multi-VLAN lab switch configuration (virtual or physical)
Network diagramming tool (draw.io or Visio)
Wireshark for verifying traffic isolation between zones

review.value: Never trust, always verify. Assume breach. Verify explicitly based on all available data points (user identity, device health, location, data sensitivity). Use least-privilege access. These principles apply whether the user is on the corporate LAN or a coffee shop Wi-Fi.

Traditional segmentation uses VLANs (layer 2) or subnets (layer 3) with firewalls between zones. Micro-segmentation uses host-based firewalls or SDN policies to enforce rules at the individual workload level. If server A does not need to talk to server B, the policy prevents it even if both are in the same VLAN.

A DMZ uses two firewalls: an external firewall allowing internet traffic to DMZ services, and an internal firewall strictly controlling DMZ-to-internal traffic. The web server in the DMZ can reach the internal database on port 5432 only, and the database cannot initiate connections to the DMZ.

Start with a rule that drops all traffic. Add explicit allow rules for each required traffic flow. This ensures that any oversight results in blocked traffic (safe) rather than allowed traffic (insecure). Every firewall rule should have a documented business justification.

class_schedule.topic: Firewall Types and Design: Stateful and Next-Generation
learning_objectives: Configure next-generation firewall rules with application awareness and SSL inspection
Implement stateful inspection and understand connection tracking tables
Design firewall rule sets following the principle of least privilege with proper ordering
Compare packet filtering, stateful inspection, application-layer gateways, and NGFW features
readings: Network Security Essentials, Ch. 4 (Firewalls)
pfSense documentation: Firewall concepts and rule management
Palo Alto Networks NGFW architecture overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Firewalls are the primary perimeter defense in network security, controlling traffic flow between security zones based on defined rules. The evolution of firewall technology reflects the increasing sophistication of both network applications and threats. First-generation packet filters examined only IP headers (source/destination IP and port), making binary allow/deny decisions for each packet independently. Stateful firewalls maintain connection tracking tables that remember the state of TCP connections and UDP flows, allowing return traffic for established connections automatically while blocking unsolicited inbound traffic. This single improvement dramatically reduced the number of rules needed and prevented many attacks that exploited the stateless nature of packet filters. Next-generation firewalls (NGFWs) add application awareness: they can identify applications regardless of port (detecting BitTorrent on port 80, for example), inspect encrypted traffic via SSL/TLS interception, integrate intrusion prevention, and leverage threat intelligence feeds for real-time blocking of known malicious indicators. Modern NGFWs from vendors like Palo Alto, Fortinet, and Checkpoint are the standard for enterprise perimeter defense. This lecture covers the technical mechanics of each firewall type, rule design principles, and the trade-offs between security granularity and performance.
key_concepts: Packet filter: examines IP header fields (src/dst IP, src/dst port, protocol), stateless, fast but limited
Stateful inspection: maintains connection state table (conntrack in Linux), automatically allows return traffic for established connections
Connection tracking: TCP connections tracked through SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK handshake, UDP tracked by timeout, ICMP by request-reply pairing
Application-layer gateway (ALG/proxy): terminates connections and inspects application-layer content (HTTP, FTP, SMTP)
NGFW features: application identification (App-ID), user identification (User-ID), SSL/TLS decryption, integrated IPS, URL filtering, threat intelligence
SSL/TLS inspection: NGFW acts as a man-in-the-middle, decrypting TLS traffic using a trusted CA certificate to inspect content, then re-encrypting
Rule ordering: firewalls evaluate rules top-to-bottom, first match wins; specific deny rules before general allow rules, implicit deny-all at the end
Rule optimization: consolidate overlapping rules, remove shadowed rules (rules that never match because an earlier rule catches all their traffic)
Firewall logging: log all denied traffic and selected allowed traffic; essential for incident response, troubleshooting, and compliance auditing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configure pfSense with three interfaces (WAN, LAN, DMZ). Create stateful firewall rules: allow HTTP/HTTPS from WAN to DMZ web server, allow SSH from LAN management subnet to all zones, allow DNS from LAN to specific external resolvers, deny all other traffic. Test each rule with nmap and curl. Then demonstrate Snort integration for deep packet inspection, detecting an SQL injection attempt in allowed HTTP traffic.
setup_requirements: pfSense VM with three network interfaces configured
Web server VM in DMZ zone for testing
nmap and curl for rule testing from various zones
Snort package installed on pfSense for IPS demonstration

review.value: Stateless packet filters require rules for both directions of a conversation (inbound request AND outbound response). Stateful firewalls track connections and automatically allow return traffic, halving the rule set and preventing attacks that send spoofed responses to closed ports.

SSL inspection allows the firewall to detect malware and data exfiltration hidden in encrypted traffic. But it breaks end-to-end encryption, requires deploying a trusted CA certificate to all clients, may break certificate pinning in applications, and raises privacy concerns for employee traffic.

A broadly permissive rule placed before a specific deny rule renders the deny rule useless (shadowed rule). A broadly restrictive rule placed before specific allow rules blocks legitimate traffic. Always order: specific deny, specific allow, general deny-all.

Packet filtering: millions of packets/second (hardware-accelerated). Stateful inspection: slightly lower due to state table lookups. NGFW with deep packet inspection: 5-20x lower throughput due to application-layer analysis. SSL inspection: 50-80% throughput reduction due to TLS decrypt/re-encrypt. Size the firewall for the inspection depth required.

class_schedule.topic: Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems: Snort and Suricata
learning_objectives: Configure Snort and Suricata IDS rules for network threat detection
Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based detection approaches
Write custom detection rules for specific attack patterns
Tune IDS to reduce false positives while maintaining detection coverage
readings: Beale et al., Snort IDS and IPS, Ch. 1-4
Suricata documentation: Rule writing and engine configuration
NIST SP 800-94: Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) are the network's immune system, detecting threats that bypass perimeter controls. While firewalls make binary allow/deny decisions based on connection metadata, IDS/IPS systems inspect the content of network traffic for malicious patterns. Signature-based detection matches traffic against a database of known attack patterns, similar to antivirus signature matching. It is highly accurate for known threats but blind to novel attacks. Anomaly-based detection builds a statistical baseline of normal network behavior and alerts on deviations, potentially catching zero-day attacks but generating more false positives. Snort, created in 1998, is the most widely deployed open-source IDS/IPS with a rule language that has become the de facto standard. Suricata, a newer alternative, supports multi-threading for higher throughput and adds protocol detection independent of port numbers. Both can operate in IDS mode (passive monitoring, generating alerts) or IPS mode (inline, actively blocking malicious traffic). The challenge in production deployment is tuning: an untuned IDS generates thousands of alerts per day, most of them false positives, leading to alert fatigue and missed real threats.
key_concepts: Signature-based detection: pattern matching against known attack signatures, high accuracy but cannot detect unknown attacks
Anomaly-based detection: statistical baseline deviation, can detect zero-days but higher false positive rate
Snort rule syntax: action protocol src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (options; including msg, content, pcre, sid, rev)
Suricata advantages over Snort: multi-threaded engine, automatic protocol detection, EVE JSON logging, Lua scripting
IDS vs IPS deployment: IDS monitors a copy of traffic (SPAN/TAP) passively; IPS sits inline and can block traffic actively
Network TAP vs SPAN port: TAP is a dedicated hardware device that copies all traffic (no packet loss); SPAN mirrors traffic on a switch port (may drop under load)
Rule categories: exploit rules (specific vulnerability), policy rules (unauthorized protocols), anomaly rules (unusual patterns)
False positive tuning: suppress rules by source/destination, adjust detection thresholds, whitelist known-good traffic patterns
Emerging Threats and Snort subscriber rules: community-maintained rule sets that are updated daily with new threat signatures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Install Suricata on a Linux VM with the Emerging Threats rule set. Write three custom rules: (1) detect HTTP requests containing SQL injection patterns (UNION SELECT), (2) detect SSH brute force (>5 connections from same source in 60 seconds using detection_filter), (3) detect DNS queries to known C2 domain patterns (high entropy subdomains). Replay pcap files containing embedded attack traffic and verify detection. Then tune the rules to eliminate false positives from legitimate traffic in the same pcap.
setup_requirements: Suricata 7.x or Snort 3 installed on Linux VM
Emerging Threats open rule set downloaded
pcap replay tools (tcpreplay) for testing
Lab pcap files with embedded attack samples and legitimate traffic

review.value: alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt"; flow:to_server,established; content:"UNION"; nocase; content:"SELECT"; nocase; distance:0; within:20; sid:1000001; rev:1;) This matches established HTTP connections containing UNION followed by SELECT within 20 bytes.

detection_filter: track by_src, count 5, seconds 60 triggers the rule only after 5 matches from the same source IP within 60 seconds. Essential for detecting brute force, port scanning, and DDoS without alerting on legitimate traffic.

An untuned IDS generates 5,000-50,000 alerts per day. Analysts can investigate perhaps 50-100. The rest are ignored, potentially hiding real attacks. Tuning is not optional: it is the difference between a useful security tool and an expensive noise generator.

IPS can block legitimate traffic (false positive blocking). A false positive in IDS mode generates an unnecessary alert; in IPS mode, it breaks a business application. Deploy in IDS mode first, tune until false positives are acceptable, then switch to IPS mode for high-confidence rules only.

learning_objectives: Configure IPSec site-to-site and remote access VPNs with IKEv2
Deploy WireGuard VPN and compare its architecture with IPSec and OpenVPN
Understand VPN cryptographic protocols: IKE phases, ESP, and authentication methods
Evaluate split tunneling risks and implement VPN security policies
readings: Network Security Essentials, Ch. 6 (VPN Technologies)
WireGuard whitepaper: A Modern, Simple, and Efficient VPN
RFC 7296: IKEv2 Protocol

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual Private Networks extend secure network connectivity across untrusted networks, enabling remote workers to access corporate resources and connecting branch offices over the internet as if they were on the same LAN. IPSec is the traditional enterprise VPN standard, providing site-to-site and remote access connectivity with rich configuration options and broad interoperability. It uses the Internet Key Exchange (IKE) protocol for authenticated key exchange and the Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) for encrypted data transport. IPSec's complexity -- two IKE phases, multiple cipher suite options, transport vs tunnel mode, and extensive configuration parameters -- makes it powerful but error-prone. WireGuard represents a fundamentally different approach: a minimal VPN protocol with ~4,000 lines of code (versus ~100,000 for OpenVPN and ~400,000 for IPSec implementations), fixed modern cryptography (no cipher suite negotiation), and a simple configuration model. WireGuard uses the Noise protocol framework for key exchange, ChaCha20-Poly1305 for authenticated encryption, and Curve25519 for Diffie-Hellman. Its stateless design with cryptokey routing means there are no connections to establish or maintain -- it simply encrypts and routes packets based on public keys. Understanding both technologies is essential because enterprises use IPSec for legacy compatibility and regulatory compliance, while WireGuard is increasingly adopted for its simplicity and performance.
key_concepts: IPSec architecture: IKE for key exchange + ESP for data encryption, operating at the network layer (IP)
IKE Phase 1 (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH in IKEv2): authenticate peers, establish encrypted IKE channel, negotiate cryptographic parameters
IKE Phase 2 (CREATE_CHILD_SA): negotiate ESP parameters (cipher, integrity, lifetime), establish IPSec Security Associations (SAs)
ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload): encrypts and authenticates IP packets, transport mode (original IP header) vs tunnel mode (new outer IP header)
IPSec modes: transport mode encrypts payload only (host-to-host), tunnel mode encrypts entire original packet (gateway-to-gateway)
WireGuard design: Noise_IK handshake, ChaCha20-Poly1305 for data, Curve25519 for DH, BLAKE2s for hashing, 1-RTT handshake
Cryptokey routing: each peer has a public key and a list of allowed IP ranges, packets are routed based on the source public key
OpenVPN: TLS-based VPN using OpenSSL, operates at layer 2 (TAP) or layer 3 (TUN), certificate authentication, mature but complex
Split tunneling: routing only corporate traffic through VPN while internet traffic goes directly; reduces VPN load but creates a bypass for data exfiltration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a WireGuard VPN between two Linux VMs simulating branch offices. Generate key pairs, configure peer allowed IPs, enable the interface, and verify encrypted connectivity. Capture traffic with tcpdump to show all data is encrypted in WireGuard UDP packets. Then set up an equivalent IPSec tunnel using strongSwan, compare the configuration complexity (WireGuard: ~10 lines, IPSec: ~50 lines), and benchmark throughput of both.
setup_requirements: Two Linux VMs with WireGuard and strongSwan installed
Network connectivity between VMs for VPN testing
tcpdump and Wireshark for traffic verification
iperf3 for VPN throughput benchmarking

review.value: WireGuard: ~4,000 LOC, fixed cryptography (no negotiation), 1-RTT handshake, ~1Gbps throughput on commodity hardware. IPSec: ~400,000 LOC, configurable cipher suites, 2-phase key exchange, hardware offload support in enterprise firewalls. Choose WireGuard for simplicity and performance; IPSec for compliance and legacy interoperability.

In tunnel mode, the entire original IP packet (header + payload) is encrypted and encapsulated in a new IP packet with the gateway's source/destination addresses. An observer sees only traffic between gateways, not the internal source/destination. This is the standard mode for site-to-site VPNs.

With split tunneling enabled, the user's internet traffic bypasses the VPN and corporate security controls (firewall, IDS, web filter). Malware on the device can exfiltrate data through the direct internet connection while appearing compliant through the VPN. Enterprise security policies often prohibit split tunneling for this reason.

WireGuard is connectionless: there is no TCP connection to break. If a mobile user switches from Wi-Fi to cellular, the endpoint IP changes but the cryptographic session continues seamlessly because authentication is based on the public key, not the IP address.

learning_objectives: Design multi-tier network architectures with proper zone isolation
Implement DMZ topologies: single-firewall, dual-firewall, and screened subnet
Apply micro-segmentation using host-based firewalls and SDN policies
Verify segmentation effectiveness through penetration testing techniques
readings: NIST SP 800-41: Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy
Network Security Essentials, Ch. 5 (Network Segmentation)
VMware NSX Micro-segmentation Design Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Network segmentation is the practice of dividing a network into smaller, isolated zones to limit the blast radius of a security breach. When an attacker compromises a system in one zone, proper segmentation prevents them from moving laterally to other zones, buying defenders time and limiting damage. The 2013 Target breach illustrates the consequence of poor segmentation: attackers compromised an HVAC vendor's credentials, accessed the corporate network, and moved laterally to the point-of-sale systems, stealing 40 million credit card numbers. Proper segmentation would have prevented the HVAC network from having any path to the payment processing systems. Traditional segmentation uses VLANs and firewalls to create zones (Internet, DMZ, Internal, Management). Modern micro-segmentation goes further, applying policies at the individual workload level using software-defined networking. A web server can talk to its application server on port 8080, the application server can talk to its database on port 5432, and no other communication is permitted -- even within the same VLAN. This lecture covers both traditional and modern segmentation approaches, DMZ architectures, and verification techniques.
key_concepts: VLAN segmentation: layer 2 isolation using 802.1Q tagging, traffic between VLANs must pass through a router/firewall
Subnet segmentation: layer 3 isolation using different IP subnets with firewall rules controlling inter-subnet traffic
DMZ single-firewall (three-legged): one firewall with three interfaces (WAN, DMZ, LAN), simpler but single point of failure
DMZ dual-firewall: external firewall between internet and DMZ, internal firewall between DMZ and LAN, defense-in-depth
Screened subnet: DMZ network between two firewalls, provides maximum isolation for public-facing services
Micro-segmentation: per-workload firewall policies using SDN (VMware NSX, Cilium) or host-based firewalls (iptables, Windows Firewall)
Network access control lists (NACLs): router-based filters that supplement firewall rules at the network layer
Segmentation verification: use nmap to scan between zones and verify only permitted ports are reachable
PCI-DSS segmentation: payment card data must be in an isolated Cardholder Data Environment (CDE), verified by quarterly segmentation testing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a dual-firewall DMZ lab with pfSense: external firewall allows HTTPS to the DMZ web server, internal firewall allows the DMZ web server to reach the internal database on port 5432 only. Verify segmentation by attempting to reach the internal network directly from the DMZ (should fail), attempting to reach the DMZ from the guest Wi-Fi zone (should fail), and confirming the web-to-database path works. Run nmap from each zone to map all reachable services and identify any segmentation violations.
setup_requirements: Two pfSense VMs for dual-firewall DMZ architecture
Web server, database server, and workstation VMs in different zones
nmap for segmentation testing and verification
tcpdump for traffic flow analysis between zones

review.value: An HVAC vendor had network access to Target's corporate network, which was not segmented from the payment processing network. Attackers used this path to reach POS systems. Lesson: every trust relationship between zones is a potential attack path. Minimize inter-zone connectivity to the absolute minimum required.

With two firewalls from different vendors, compromising one firewall (e.g., via a firmware vulnerability) does not give access to the internal network -- the attacker must also compromise the second, different firewall. This diversity-in-depth principle applies to all critical security controls.

Attackers may attempt VLAN hopping via double-tagging (encapsulating a packet in two 802.1Q tags) or switch spoofing (pretending to be a trunk port). Mitigations: disable unused ports, disable auto-trunking, explicitly configure access ports, use private VLANs.

Segmentation is only as good as its verification. Run monthly automated scans between every pair of zones to confirm only authorized traffic flows. PCI-DSS requires quarterly segmentation penetration testing by a qualified assessor. Document every authorized inter-zone traffic flow.

class_schedule.topic: Wireless Security: WPA2, WPA3, and Attack Vectors
learning_objectives: Analyze the security mechanisms of WPA2 (Personal and Enterprise) and WPA3
Understand wireless attack vectors: KRACK, evil twin, deauthentication, and rogue AP
Implement wireless security best practices for enterprise deployments
Configure 802.1X/RADIUS authentication for enterprise wireless
readings: Network Security Essentials, Ch. 7 (Wireless Security)
Vanhoef & Piessens, Key Reinstallation Attacks (KRACK, 2017)
Wi-Fi Alliance WPA3 specification overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Wireless networks transmit data over radio waves that anyone within range can receive, making them inherently more exposed than wired networks. The security of wireless communication depends entirely on the encryption protocol used between the client and access point. WPA2, the current standard since 2004, uses AES-CCMP for encryption and supports two authentication modes: Personal (pre-shared key/PSK, suitable for home use) and Enterprise (802.1X/RADIUS authentication with per-user credentials, required for corporate environments). The KRACK attack (Key Reinstallation Attack) in 2017 demonstrated that WPA2's four-way handshake was vulnerable to nonce reuse when an attacker replayed handshake message 3, allowing decryption of traffic. WPA3, released in 2018, addresses this and other weaknesses: Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE, also known as Dragonfly) replaces the PSK four-way handshake with a zero-knowledge proof that resists offline dictionary attacks. WPA3 Enterprise adds 192-bit security mode with stronger cipher suites. Beyond protocol vulnerabilities, wireless networks face practical attacks: evil twin APs that mimic legitimate networks to intercept traffic, deauthentication attacks that disconnect clients to force handshake capture, and rogue APs that provide unauthorized network access. Enterprise wireless security requires not just strong encryption but also proper authentication, monitoring for rogue APs, and client-side protections.
key_concepts: WPA2-Personal: 4-way handshake derives pairwise transient key (PTK) from pre-shared key (PSK), SSID, and nonces
WPA2-Enterprise: 802.1X authentication with RADIUS server, EAP methods (EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-TTLS), per-user session keys
AES-CCMP: Counter mode with CBC-MAC Protocol, provides confidentiality and integrity for wireless frames
KRACK attack: replaying handshake message 3 causes the client to reinstall the same key with a reset nonce, enabling decryption of subsequent frames
WPA3-SAE (Dragonfly): password-authenticated key exchange where both sides prove knowledge of the password without revealing it, resistant to offline dictionary attacks
WPA3 forward secrecy: compromise of the PSK does not allow decryption of previously captured traffic (unlike WPA2 where PSK + captured handshake = decryption)
Evil twin attack: attacker creates an AP with the same SSID as a legitimate network, victims connect and send traffic through the attacker
Deauthentication attack: attacker sends forged deauth frames to disconnect clients, forcing them to reconnect and exposing the WPA2 handshake for offline cracking
Wireless IDS (WIDS): monitors the RF spectrum for rogue APs, evil twins, and deauthentication attacks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a WPA2-Enterprise network with a FreeRADIUS server and EAP-TLS authentication (certificate-based). Demonstrate a deauthentication attack using aireplay-ng, capture the WPA2 four-way handshake with airodump-ng, and attempt to crack the PSK with hashcat (on a weak PSK). Then configure WPA3-SAE on a compatible access point and show that the captured handshake cannot be used for offline cracking due to SAE's zero-knowledge proof.
setup_requirements: Wireless access points supporting WPA2 and WPA3
FreeRADIUS server for 802.1X authentication
Aircrack-ng suite for wireless security testing
Wireless adapter capable of monitor mode and packet injection

review.value: KRACK is a client-side vulnerability patched by not reinstalling the key when message 3 is retransmitted. All major OS vendors released patches in late 2017. WPA3's SAE handshake is fundamentally immune to this attack because it uses a different key exchange mechanism.

With a captured four-way handshake, an attacker can perform offline dictionary/brute-force attacks against the PSK. A weak PSK (dictionary word, short) can be cracked in minutes with GPU-accelerated hashcat. WPA3-SAE prevents offline attacks: each guess requires an interactive exchange with the AP.

Each user has individual credentials (username/password or certificate). Compromise of one user does not affect others. Users can be individually revoked. Session keys are unique per user. Logging provides per-user accountability. This is why WPA2-Enterprise is mandatory for corporate networks.

Client-side: verify server certificate in EAP-TLS/PEAP (do not click 'trust' for unknown certificates). Network-side: deploy Wireless Intrusion Detection System (WIDS) to detect APs with your SSID but unauthorized MAC addresses or radio characteristics.

learning_objectives: Implement 802.1X port-based network access control on wired and wireless networks
Configure RADIUS authentication with EAP methods for network admission
Design NAC policies for device posture assessment and quarantine
Evaluate NAC deployment challenges and bypass techniques
readings: IEEE 802.1X-2020 Standard overview
NIST SP 800-127: Guide to Securing WiMAX Wireless Communications (NAC sections)
FreeRADIUS Administrator's Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Network Access Control (NAC) ensures that only authorized and compliant devices connect to the network. Without NAC, anyone who plugs an Ethernet cable into a wall jack or connects to the corporate Wi-Fi gains network access, potentially introducing compromised or unmanaged devices. IEEE 802.1X is the standard for port-based network access control. When a device connects to an 802.1X-enabled switch port, the port starts in an unauthorized state, dropping all traffic except EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) frames. The switch acts as an authenticator, relaying EAP messages between the supplicant (client device) and the authentication server (RADIUS). Only after successful authentication does the port transition to authorized state and allow traffic. The RADIUS server can assign the authenticated device to a specific VLAN based on user role, device type, or posture assessment results. Beyond authentication, modern NAC solutions assess device posture: is the OS patched? Is antivirus running and up to date? Is disk encryption enabled? Devices that fail posture checks are placed in a quarantine VLAN with access only to remediation resources. NAC is a critical component of zero trust architecture, ensuring that network access is granted based on verified identity and device health rather than physical location.
key_concepts: 802.1X components: supplicant (client software), authenticator (switch/AP acting as gatekeeper), authentication server (RADIUS)
EAP methods: EAP-TLS (mutual certificate auth, strongest), PEAP (server cert + password in TLS tunnel), EAP-TTLS (similar to PEAP, more flexible)
RADIUS protocol: centralized authentication and accounting, attributes in Access-Accept determine VLAN assignment and access policies
Dynamic VLAN assignment: RADIUS returns VLAN ID in the Access-Accept message, switch places the port in the assigned VLAN
MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB): for devices without 802.1X supplicant (printers, IoT), authenticate based on MAC address (weaker, MAC can be spoofed)
Device posture assessment: check OS version, patch level, antivirus status, disk encryption, firewall state before granting full access
Quarantine VLAN: non-compliant devices receive limited network access (only remediation servers) until posture issues are resolved
Guest VLAN: unauthenticated or visitor devices receive internet-only access, isolated from corporate resources
NAC bypass techniques: MAC spoofing for MAB, 802.1X relay attacks, exploiting authenticated sessions with physical access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configure 802.1X on a managed switch with FreeRADIUS: create user certificates for EAP-TLS, configure the switch port as an 802.1X authenticator, demonstrate authentication success (port authorized, VLAN assignment) and failure (port stays unauthorized, traffic dropped). Then demonstrate MAC Authentication Bypass for a network printer and show how easy MAC spoofing is -- justifying why MAB should be restricted to low-security zones.
setup_requirements: Managed switch with 802.1X support (or OpenVSwitch)
FreeRADIUS server with EAP-TLS configured
Client with wpa_supplicant for 802.1X
Network printer or IoT device for MAB demonstration

review.value: Both client and server present X.509 certificates. No passwords to steal, no hashes to crack. Certificate revocation immediately disables access. This is the gold standard for enterprise NAC but requires PKI infrastructure to issue and manage client certificates.

The same physical port can place different devices in different VLANs based on who authenticates. An IT admin gets placed in the management VLAN, a regular employee in the corporate VLAN, a contractor in the restricted VLAN -- all using the same physical switch port.

IoT devices without 802.1X supplicants require MAB (weak). BYOD devices may not support corporate certificates. Legacy equipment may not support any NAC. Deployment must be phased: monitor mode first (log but do not enforce), then enforce for one zone at a time.

802.1X provides network-layer access control (authenticate at connection time). Zero trust requires continuous verification throughout the session. 802.1X is a component of zero trust but not sufficient alone: a device that was healthy at authentication time may become compromised during the session.

learning_objectives: Classify DDoS attacks by type: volumetric, protocol, and application-layer
Design multi-layer DDoS mitigation architectures using ISP, CDN, and on-premise controls
Implement rate limiting, SYN cookies, and web application firewall rules for DDoS defense
Analyze real-world DDoS attacks and their mitigation strategies
readings: Network Security Essentials, Ch. 8 (DDoS)
Cloudflare DDoS Threat Landscape Report (latest)
NIST SP 800-189: Resilient Interdomain Traffic Exchange

lecture_notes.introduction: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks aim to make a service unavailable by overwhelming it with traffic from many sources. They are the most common and most visible type of cyber attack, affecting organizations of all sizes. DDoS attacks fall into three categories based on their target. Volumetric attacks flood the network link with massive traffic (UDP floods, DNS amplification, NTP amplification), measured in Gbps. Protocol attacks exhaust server state tables and processing capacity (SYN floods, fragmented packet attacks), measured in packets per second. Application-layer attacks target the application with legitimate-looking requests that are expensive to process (HTTP floods, Slowloris, DNS query floods), measured in requests per second. Each category requires different mitigation techniques. Volumetric attacks must be absorbed upstream (ISP scrubbing, CDN, Anycast) because no on-premise equipment can handle terabits of traffic. Protocol attacks are mitigated with SYN cookies, connection rate limiting, and stateless packet processing. Application-layer attacks require WAF rules, CAPTCHA challenges, and behavioral analysis. The largest recorded DDoS attacks have exceeded 3.47 Tbps (Microsoft Azure, 2021), demonstrating that on-premise-only defense is insufficient for any internet-facing service.
key_concepts: Volumetric attacks: UDP flood, DNS amplification (50-100x amplification factor), NTP monlist amplification, memcached amplification (50,000x factor)
Protocol attacks: SYN flood (exhaust connection state table), ACK flood, fragmented packet attacks, Ping of Death
Application-layer attacks: HTTP GET/POST flood, Slowloris (keep connections open with partial requests), DNS query flood targeting specific records
SYN cookies: stateless SYN-ACK response that encodes connection state in the initial sequence number, no state table entry until the ACK is received
Rate limiting: token bucket or leaky bucket algorithms to cap requests per source IP or per URL path
Anycast routing: same IP address announced from multiple points of presence, traffic automatically distributed geographically
ISP upstream filtering: BGP Flowspec or RTBH (Remote Triggered Black Hole) to filter attack traffic at the ISP level before it reaches your network
CDN absorption: services like Cloudflare, AWS Shield, and Akamai have terabits of capacity to absorb volumetric attacks
BGP hijacking for DDoS: attackers can announce your IP prefix to redirect traffic; RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) prevents this
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulate three types of DDoS attacks in the lab: (1) SYN flood using hping3, observe the target's connection table filling up with half-open connections, then enable SYN cookies and show the server remains responsive. (2) HTTP flood using slowhttptest (Slowloris), observe the web server running out of worker threads, then configure connection timeout and max-connection-per-IP limits. (3) DNS amplification using a misconfigured open resolver, measure the amplification factor, then configure the resolver to refuse recursive queries from external sources.
setup_requirements: Lab network with target web server and DNS server
hping3 for SYN flood simulation
slowhttptest for application-layer attack simulation
Wireshark for analyzing attack traffic patterns

review.value: A DNS query for ANY on a domain with large TXT records can be ~60 bytes but produce a ~4,000 byte response (67x amplification). With a spoofed source IP (the victim), the attacker sends 1 Gbps of queries and the victim receives 67 Gbps of responses. This is why open DNS resolvers are a global threat.

Instead of storing connection state in a table on SYN receipt, the server encodes the state (source IP, port, MSS, timestamp) into the initial sequence number (ISN) of the SYN-ACK. When the client's ACK arrives, the server decodes the ISN to reconstruct the connection state. No state is stored until the three-way handshake completes.

Layer 1: ISP/upstream filtering for volumetric (Flowspec, RTBH). Layer 2: CDN/Anycast for network-level absorption. Layer 3: On-premise firewall/IPS for protocol attacks. Layer 4: WAF with rate limiting and behavioral analysis for application-layer attacks. No single layer handles all attack types.

BCP38/BCP84: ISPs should implement ingress filtering to block packets with spoofed source IPs. If all ISPs implemented this, amplification attacks would be impossible. Progress is slow: ~30% of ISPs still allow source IP spoofing.

learning_objectives: Explain DNS security vulnerabilities: cache poisoning, DNS spoofing, DNS tunneling
Implement DNSSEC to provide DNS response authentication and integrity
Deploy DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) and DNS-over-TLS (DoT) for query privacy
Design a secure DNS architecture for an enterprise network
readings: NIST SP 800-81: Secure Domain Name System Deployment Guide
RFC 4033-4035: DNSSEC specifications
RFC 8484: DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH)

lecture_notes.introduction: DNS is the internet's phone book, translating domain names to IP addresses for every network connection. It is also one of the least secured critical internet protocols: designed in 1983 without authentication or encryption, DNS traffic is transmitted in plaintext UDP packets that can be intercepted, modified, and spoofed. DNS cache poisoning (Kaminsky attack, 2008) allows an attacker to inject false DNS records into a resolver's cache, redirecting users to malicious servers. DNS spoofing achieves the same effect by responding to queries faster than the legitimate server. DNS tunneling encodes data in DNS queries and responses to exfiltrate information or establish command-and-control channels through firewalls that allow DNS traffic. DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records, allowing resolvers to verify that responses are authentic and unmodified. However, DNSSEC does not encrypt queries, leaving privacy exposed. DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) and DNS-over-TLS (DoT) encrypt DNS queries between the client and resolver, preventing eavesdropping and manipulation of queries in transit. A comprehensive DNS security strategy combines DNSSEC for response authentication, encrypted DNS for query privacy, DNS firewalling for threat blocking, and DNS monitoring for anomaly detection.
key_concepts: DNS cache poisoning (Kaminsky attack): attacker sends many forged responses to a resolver's query, hoping one matches the query ID and port; if successful, the false record is cached and served to all users
DNSSEC chain of trust: root zone signs TLD keys (e.g., .com), TLD signs domain keys, domain signs individual record sets (RRSIG, DNSKEY, DS records)
DNSSEC validation: resolver verifies RRSIG signature on each DNS response using the DNSKEY, traces the chain to a trusted root key anchor
DNS-over-TLS (DoT): DNS queries encrypted with TLS on port 853, provides privacy between client and resolver
DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH): DNS queries carried as HTTPS requests on port 443, indistinguishable from normal web traffic
DNS firewall (RPZ - Response Policy Zone): resolver rewrites DNS responses based on threat intelligence, blocking queries to known malicious domains
DNS tunneling detection: monitor for high entropy in subdomain labels, unusually long queries, high query volume per domain, and TXT record abuse
Split-horizon DNS: internal DNS returns private IP addresses; external DNS returns public addresses; prevents internal topology leakage
DANE (DNS-based Authentication of Named Entities): TLSA records in DNSSEC-signed zones bind TLS certificates to domain names, reducing dependence on CAs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a DNSSEC-signed zone using BIND9: generate KSK and ZSK key pairs, sign the zone file, configure a validating resolver that checks DNSSEC signatures. Demonstrate cache poisoning attempt against an unsigned zone (successful) and against a signed zone (rejected by the validating resolver). Then configure DNS-over-TLS on the resolver using Unbound and verify that DNS queries are encrypted with tcpdump.
setup_requirements: BIND9 authoritative DNS server for DNSSEC signing
Unbound recursive resolver with DNSSEC validation and DoT
dnssec-verify tools for zone validation
tcpdump/Wireshark for verifying DNS encryption

review.value: As of 2024, ~95% of TLDs are signed with DNSSEC, but only ~30% of resolvers validate DNSSEC signatures. A signed zone is useless if the resolver does not check signatures. Google DNS (8.8.8.8) and Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) both perform DNSSEC validation.

DoH encrypts DNS queries, improving user privacy but preventing network-level DNS monitoring and filtering. Enterprise security teams cannot inspect DoH traffic to detect malware DNS queries. This tension between privacy and visibility is one of the most debated topics in network security.

Normal DNS: short queries (avg 20 chars), low query rate, common record types (A, AAAA). Tunneling: long queries (60+ chars), high entropy in subdomains (looks like base64), high query rate to single domain, unusual record types (TXT, NULL). Monitor these metrics for detection.

RPZ allows a resolver to rewrite DNS responses based on threat intelligence feeds. Queries for known malicious domains return NXDOMAIN or redirect to a block page. This provides DNS-layer protection for all devices on the network without requiring client-side software.

class_schedule.topic: Email Security: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC
learning_objectives: Configure SPF, DKIM, and DMARC to authenticate email and prevent spoofing
Analyze email headers to trace message routing and verify authentication results
Understand phishing attack vectors and email-based threat mitigation
Deploy email security gateways for malware and spam filtering
readings: RFC 7208: Sender Policy Framework (SPF)
RFC 6376: DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM)
RFC 7489: Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance (DMARC)

lecture_notes.introduction: Email is the primary attack vector for phishing, business email compromise, and malware delivery, responsible for over 90% of successful cyber attacks. The SMTP protocol, designed in 1982, has no built-in sender authentication: anyone can send an email claiming to be from any address, making email spoofing trivially easy. Three complementary technologies address this. SPF (Sender Policy Framework) publishes a DNS TXT record listing the IP addresses authorized to send email for a domain. Receiving servers check if the sending server's IP is in the SPF record. DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) cryptographically signs the email headers and body with the sending domain's private key, with the public key published in DNS. Receiving servers verify the signature to confirm the email was not modified in transit and was sent by an authorized server. DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance) ties SPF and DKIM together with a policy that tells receiving servers what to do with emails that fail authentication: none (monitor only), quarantine (send to spam), or reject (drop the email). DMARC also provides reporting so domain owners can see who is sending email using their domain. Together, SPF+DKIM+DMARC provide a robust defense against email spoofing, but adoption remains incomplete: as of 2024, only ~50% of domains have a DMARC record, and far fewer have a reject policy.
key_concepts: SPF: DNS TXT record specifying authorized sending IPs, e.g., 'v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ip4:203.0.113.0/24 -all'
SPF mechanisms: ip4/ip6 (specific IPs), include (delegate to another SPF record), a/mx (domain's A or MX records), -all (hard fail) vs ~all (soft fail)
DKIM: RSA or Ed25519 signature over email headers (From, To, Subject, Date) and body, public key in DNS TXT record at selector._domainkey.domain
DKIM signature fields: d= (signing domain), s= (selector for key lookup), b= (signature), bh= (body hash), h= (signed headers)
DMARC: DNS TXT record at _dmarc.domain specifying policy (p=none/quarantine/reject), alignment requirements, and reporting addresses
DMARC alignment: the From header domain must match the SPF authenticated domain and/or the DKIM signing domain
Email header analysis: tracing Received headers to identify the actual sending server, checking Authentication-Results for SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass/fail
Business Email Compromise (BEC): attacker spoofs or compromises an executive's email to authorize fraudulent wire transfers (>$2.7 billion annual losses per FBI IC3)
Secure Email Gateway (SEG): appliance/service that filters inbound/outbound email for spam, malware, phishing links, and data loss prevention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configure SPF, DKIM, and DMARC for a test domain: publish the SPF TXT record, generate DKIM key pair and publish the public key in DNS, configure the mail server to sign outgoing messages, publish a DMARC record with p=reject. Send a test email and examine the Authentication-Results header showing SPF pass, DKIM pass, and DMARC pass. Then send a spoofed email from an unauthorized server and show it being rejected by a receiving server that checks DMARC.
setup_requirements: DNS zone control for a test domain
Postfix or similar mail server with DKIM signing (OpenDKIM)
Email client for sending and receiving test messages
Header analysis tool (MX Toolbox or manual header inspection)

review.value: SPF evaluation is limited to 10 DNS lookups (includes, a, mx mechanisms). Exceeding this limit causes a permanent error (permerror), and the SPF check fails. Large organizations with many email services must carefully manage their SPF records to stay within this limit.

A valid DKIM signature remains valid even if the email is forwarded, because the signature covers the content, not the envelope. An attacker who receives a legitimately signed email can resend it to other recipients with a valid DKIM signature. DMARC alignment and ARC (Authenticated Received Chain) mitigate this.

Start with p=none to collect reports without affecting delivery. Analyze reports to identify all legitimate email sources. Ensure all sources are covered by SPF and DKIM. Move to p=quarantine, monitor for false positives. Finally, move to p=reject for full protection. This process typically takes 3-6 months.

DMARC prevents exact-domain spoofing but not lookalike domains (examp1e.com vs example.com). Additional defenses: train users to verify wire transfer requests via phone call, implement dual-approval for financial transactions, use domain monitoring services to detect lookalike domain registrations.

learning_objectives: Design a centralized log collection architecture with proper log sources and retention
Write SIEM correlation rules to detect multi-stage attack patterns
Implement Splunk or ELK-based security monitoring dashboards
Understand the role of SIEM in incident detection and compliance reporting
readings: NIST SP 800-92: Guide to Computer Security Log Management
Chuvakin et al., Logging and Log Management, Ch. 1-5
Elastic SIEM documentation and detection rules

lecture_notes.introduction: Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) is the central nervous system of a security operations center. It collects log data from every device on the network -- firewalls, IDS/IPS, servers, endpoints, applications, cloud services -- normalizes it into a common format, stores it for historical analysis, and applies correlation rules to detect security incidents in real time. Without a SIEM, security analysts must log into dozens of individual systems to investigate an incident, manually correlating timestamps and IP addresses across different log formats. A single brute-force attack might generate a firewall deny log, an IDS alert, an authentication failure log, and an account lockout event. Only by correlating these events from different sources can the analyst understand the full attack. SIEM correlation rules detect patterns that no single log source reveals: a successful login from an IP that failed authentication 50 times in the previous hour, a user downloading 10GB of data at 3am after a VPN login from an unusual country, or a server making DNS queries to a domain registered yesterday. Modern SIEMs also support User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA), which builds baselines of normal behavior for each user and device and alerts on deviations without requiring predefined rules. The two most common open-source SIEM platforms are the ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) and Wazuh; Splunk dominates the commercial market.
key_concepts: Log sources: firewalls (denied/allowed connections), IDS/IPS (alerts), authentication systems (login success/failure), DNS (queries), proxies (URL access), endpoints (process execution)
Log normalization: converting diverse log formats (syslog, JSON, Windows Event Log, CEF) into a common schema for correlation
Correlation rules: multi-event patterns that indicate security incidents, e.g., '5 failed logins followed by 1 successful login from same source within 10 minutes'
SIEM architecture: log collectors (agents/forwarders) -> log aggregator (Logstash/Fluentd) -> storage (Elasticsearch) -> analysis (Kibana/Splunk dashboards)
Alert prioritization: enrich alerts with context (asset criticality, user role, threat intelligence) to assign severity and reduce alert fatigue
User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA): machine learning baseline of normal user behavior, alerts on anomalous actions (unusual login time, unusual data access)
Log retention: compliance requirements dictate retention periods (PCI-DSS: 1 year online/3 years archive, HIPAA: 6 years, SOX: 7 years)
Common SIEM use cases: brute force detection, lateral movement detection, data exfiltration detection, insider threat detection, compliance reporting
Detection engineering: writing, testing, and maintaining detection rules as an ongoing process, not a one-time configuration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy an ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) and ingest logs from pfSense (firewall), Suricata (IDS), and a Linux web server (Apache access logs and auth logs). Write three correlation rules: (1) detect brute force: 10+ failed SSH logins from one IP in 5 minutes, (2) detect potential lateral movement: successful RDP login from an internal IP that was the target of a prior IDS alert, (3) detect data exfiltration: outbound transfer >1GB to a non-business destination. Build a Kibana dashboard showing these alerts with drill-down capability.
setup_requirements: ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) on a dedicated VM
Filebeat agents on pfSense, Suricata, and web server
Sample log data with embedded attack patterns
Kibana dashboard templates for security monitoring

review.value: Effective rules combine multiple conditions: source IP, time window, event count, event type sequence. Start with high-confidence rules (many failed logins followed by success is almost always an attack) and expand. Every rule must be tested with both attack data (true positive) and normal data (verify no false positive).

A typical enterprise SIEM ingests 10,000-100,000 events per second. Without tuning, it generates hundreds of alerts per day. The goal is not zero alerts but actionable alerts: every alert should require investigation. Achieving this requires months of tuning and continuous maintenance.

If an attacker compromises a system, they will attempt to delete or modify logs. Centralized logging to a SIEM that the attacker cannot access preserves the evidence. Log forwarding should be real-time (not batched) to minimize the window for log deletion.

Map SIEM detection rules to MITRE ATT&CK techniques to identify coverage gaps. If you have rules for Initial Access and Lateral Movement but none for Persistence or Exfiltration, attackers can achieve those objectives undetected. Use ATT&CK heatmaps to visualize and prioritize detection development.

class_schedule.topic: Network Traffic Analysis and Threat Hunting
learning_objectives: Analyze network traffic captures to identify malicious activity patterns
Use Zeek (Bro) for protocol analysis and metadata extraction from pcap files
Develop threat hunting hypotheses based on MITRE ATT&CK techniques
Identify command-and-control traffic patterns in network metadata
readings: Bejtlich, The Practice of Network Security Monitoring, Ch. 3-5
Zeek documentation: Log files and scripting
MITRE ATT&CK: Command and Control tactics

lecture_notes.introduction: Network traffic analysis goes beyond IDS alerting by proactively examining network communications for signs of compromise. While IDS relies on known signatures, traffic analysis looks at behavioral patterns that indicate adversary activity regardless of the specific tools or exploits used. Threat hunting is the proactive practice of searching for threats that have evaded automated detection, guided by hypotheses about adversary behavior. A threat hunter might hypothesize that an advanced attacker is using DNS tunneling for command and control, then examine DNS logs for indicators: unusually long subdomain labels, high entropy in query names, excessive query volume to a single domain, or large TXT record responses. Zeek (formerly Bro) is the primary tool for network traffic analysis, generating structured logs from network traffic: conn.log records every connection with timing, bytes transferred, and protocol; dns.log captures every DNS query and response; http.log records HTTP transactions with URIs and user agents; ssl.log records TLS handshake details including certificate information. These logs provide the raw material for both automated detection and manual threat hunting.
key_concepts: Full packet capture vs metadata: full capture preserves entire packet content (expensive storage) vs metadata (connection records, DNS queries, HTTP headers) which is cheaper and often sufficient
Zeek log types: conn.log (connection records), dns.log (DNS queries), http.log (HTTP requests), ssl.log (TLS handshakes), files.log (file transfers)
Zeek scripting: custom analyzers that extract specific indicators from traffic, e.g., detecting JA3 fingerprints or unusual user agents
JA3/JA3S fingerprinting: hashing TLS ClientHello parameters to fingerprint TLS client applications (malware often has distinctive JA3 fingerprints)
Beaconing detection: identifying periodic callbacks (e.g., every 60 seconds with jitter) characteristic of C2 communication
DNS analytics for threat detection: high entropy subdomains, TXT record tunneling, domain generation algorithms (DGA), newly registered domains
Network baseline: statistical profile of normal traffic patterns (protocols, volumes, timing, destinations) that enables anomaly detection
Threat hunting process: hypothesis -> data collection -> analysis -> findings -> new detections (rules/signatures)
MITRE ATT&CK network techniques: T1071 (Application Layer Protocol for C2), T1048 (Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol), T1572 (Protocol Tunneling)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Process a malicious pcap file through Zeek to generate logs. Analyze conn.log to identify a suspicious long-duration connection with periodic data transfers (beaconing). Examine dns.log for high-entropy subdomain queries (DNS tunneling indicators). Check ssl.log for TLS connections to servers with self-signed or recently issued certificates. Extract JA3 fingerprints and compare against a known-malicious JA3 hash database. Build a timeline of the attack from initial compromise through data exfiltration using only network metadata.
setup_requirements: Zeek installed on Linux VM with pcap processing capability
Sample pcap files with embedded C2 and exfiltration traffic
JA3/JA3S fingerprint database
Python or jq scripts for log analysis

review.value: C2 malware calls home at regular intervals (e.g., every 60 seconds with 10% jitter). Calculate the standard deviation of inter-connection times for each destination. Low standard deviation relative to the interval indicates beaconing. Normal traffic (web browsing) has high variance; beaconing has low variance.

JA3 hashes the cipher suites, extensions, and elliptic curves from TLS ClientHello into a 32-character MD5 hash. Each TLS client (browser, malware, tool) has a characteristic fingerprint. This detects malware even when it connects to legitimate-looking domains because its TLS implementation differs from standard browsers.

Good hypotheses are specific, testable, and based on adversary behavior: 'An attacker is exfiltrating data via DNS TXT records to domains registered in the last 30 days.' This leads to specific data queries: DNS TXT responses >500 bytes to domains with registration date <30 days. Bad hypothesis: 'something suspicious is happening.'

Network metadata provides timestamps for every connection and DNS query. By sorting all events by time and filtering by the compromised host's IP, you can reconstruct the attack timeline: initial infection (exploit delivery), C2 establishment (first beacon), reconnaissance (internal scanning), lateral movement (RDP/SMB to other hosts), exfiltration (large outbound transfers).

learning_objectives: Design a zero trust architecture for an enterprise using NIST SP 800-207 framework
Implement identity-based access control that replaces network-based trust
Deploy software-defined perimeter (SDP) for application-level access control
Evaluate zero trust maturity models and plan a migration from traditional perimeter security
readings: NIST SP 800-207: Zero Trust Architecture
Google BeyondCorp: A New Approach to Enterprise Security
CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model

lecture_notes.introduction: Zero trust is not a product or technology but an architectural approach that eliminates implicit trust from the network. The traditional enterprise model assumed that users and devices inside the corporate firewall were trustworthy -- a castle-and-moat approach where the focus was on strengthening the perimeter. Zero trust recognizes that this assumption is fundamentally flawed: employees' devices get compromised, credentials get stolen, and VPN connections extend the trusted perimeter to every coffee shop and home office. Google pioneered the zero trust approach with BeyondCorp after the Aurora operation (2009), where Chinese state-sponsored attackers compromised Google's internal network through a targeted phishing campaign. BeyondCorp eliminated the distinction between internal and external networks: every access request -- regardless of source network -- is authenticated against user identity, authorized against access policies, and encrypted. The user's device is assessed for health (OS version, patch level, encryption status) before access is granted. Access decisions are made per-request, not per-session, enabling continuous verification. The NIST SP 800-207 framework formalizes zero trust into seven tenets and describes three deployment approaches: enhanced identity governance, micro-segmentation, and software-defined perimeter. This lecture covers the architecture, implementation challenges, and a practical migration path from traditional perimeter security to zero trust.
key_concepts: NIST Zero Trust tenets: all data sources and computing services are resources; all communication is secured regardless of network location; access is granted per-session; access is determined by dynamic policy
Identity as the new perimeter: user identity + device identity + context (time, location, behavior) determine access, not network location
Software-Defined Perimeter (SDP): application infrastructure is invisible to unauthorized users; no open ports until authentication and authorization are complete
Policy Decision Point (PDP): evaluates access requests against policy using user identity, device health, resource sensitivity, and threat intelligence
Policy Enforcement Point (PEP): gateway/proxy that enforces PDP decisions by allowing or denying access to specific resources
Device trust assessment: verify OS version, patch level, disk encryption, antivirus status, MDM enrollment before granting access
Continuous verification: re-evaluate access decisions during the session; revoke access if device health changes or anomalous behavior detected
Micro-segmentation in zero trust: every application is a segment; access to each application is individually authenticated and authorized
Google BeyondCorp: no VPN required; employees access applications through an identity-aware proxy regardless of physical location
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a simplified zero trust access model: deploy an identity-aware proxy (e.g., Pomerium or OAuth2 Proxy) in front of a web application. Configure the proxy to require user authentication via OIDC, check device health (OS version) via a device trust service, and enforce role-based access policies. Demonstrate that the application is inaccessible without authentication (no open ports visible), that authenticated users with healthy devices get access, and that access is denied when the device fails a health check.
setup_requirements: Identity-aware proxy (Pomerium, OAuth2 Proxy, or similar)
OIDC identity provider (Keycloak or Auth0 for testing)
Web application behind the proxy for access control testing
Device health assessment service (simplified implementation)

review.value: After BeyondCorp, Google employees access internal applications from any network (home, coffee shop, hotel) without VPN. The identity-aware proxy verifies user identity, device health, and access policy for every request. This made remote work trivially secure and eliminated the VPN as a single point of failure and attack target.

Zero trust is not a rip-and-replace migration. Start by identifying critical applications and data. Implement strong identity for users and devices. Deploy identity-aware proxies for critical applications. Add device health assessment. Gradually migrate applications from VPN-based to proxy-based access. This typically takes 2-5 years for large organizations.

VPN grants broad network access: once connected, the user can reach any resource on the allowed network segments. SDP grants application-specific access: the user can access only the specific applications they are authorized for, and the network infrastructure is invisible. SDP eliminates lateral movement by design.

True continuous verification requires real-time signals: device health changes, new threat intelligence, user behavior anomalies, time-based policy changes. This requires tight integration between endpoint detection, identity provider, policy engine, and enforcement points. Most organizations implement periodic (not continuous) verification as a pragmatic starting point.

learning_objectives: Design secure network architectures in AWS, Azure, and GCP cloud environments
Implement cloud-native security controls: security groups, NACLs, VPC peering, and PrivateLink
Understand the shared responsibility model for network security in cloud deployments
Evaluate cloud network security posture using automated assessment tools
readings: AWS Well-Architected Framework: Security Pillar (Networking section)
NIST SP 800-144: Guidelines on Security and Privacy in Public Cloud Computing
CIS Benchmarks for AWS/Azure/GCP networking

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud computing has fundamentally changed network security. Traditional security architectures assumed physical control over network infrastructure: you owned the switches, routers, and firewalls. In cloud environments, the network infrastructure is managed by the cloud provider, and security is implemented through software-defined controls. The shared responsibility model defines the boundary: the cloud provider secures the infrastructure (physical network, hypervisor, storage), while the customer secures what they deploy on it (security groups, NACLs, VPC design, application security). This division means that many traditional network security concepts translate directly to cloud equivalents but with different implementations. Security groups replace host-based firewalls. VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) replaces physical network segmentation. VPC peering replaces WAN links between offices. PrivateLink provides private connectivity to services without internet exposure. Cloud-native network security has advantages over traditional approaches: every security group rule is software-defined and version-controlled, changes propagate in seconds rather than requiring manual firewall configuration, and cloud providers offer native DDoS protection (AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection). However, cloud also introduces new risks: misconfigured security groups are the leading cause of cloud data breaches (Capital One 2019: misconfigured WAF allowed SSRF to access S3 buckets), and the rapid pace of cloud development means security teams struggle to keep up with infrastructure changes.
key_concepts: Shared responsibility model: cloud provider secures infrastructure (network, hypervisor), customer secures configuration (security groups, encryption, access control)
VPC (Virtual Private Cloud): isolated virtual network with customer-defined IP ranges, subnets, route tables, and internet gateways
Security groups: stateful virtual firewalls applied to individual instances, allow rules only (implicit deny), evaluated as a group (all rules combined)
Network ACLs: stateless subnet-level firewall rules, support both allow and deny, processed in order with first match
Public vs private subnets: public subnets have route to internet gateway, private subnets route through NAT gateway for outbound-only internet access
VPC peering: private connectivity between VPCs (same or different accounts/regions) without internet transit, non-transitive (A<->B, B<->C does not imply A<->C)
AWS PrivateLink / Azure Private Endpoint: access cloud services (S3, RDS) through private IP addresses within the VPC, traffic never traverses the internet
Cloud-native DDoS protection: AWS Shield Standard (automatic, free), Shield Advanced (managed response), Azure DDoS Protection, GCP Cloud Armor
Cloud security posture management (CSPM): automated scanning for misconfigurations (open security groups, public S3 buckets, unencrypted databases)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a secure VPC architecture in AWS: create a VPC with public subnet (load balancer), private application subnet (web servers), and private data subnet (RDS database). Configure security groups with least-privilege rules (ALB allows 443 from internet, web servers allow 8080 from ALB only, RDS allows 5432 from web servers only). Verify segmentation by attempting direct database access from the internet (should fail) and from the web servers (should succeed). Run AWS Config rules to detect any security group allowing 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports.
setup_requirements: AWS account with VPC and EC2 access (free tier sufficient)
Terraform or CloudFormation templates for reproducible infrastructure
AWS CLI for security group management and testing
ScoutSuite or Prowler for cloud security assessment

review.value: A misconfigured WAF on an EC2 instance allowed server-side request forgery (SSRF), enabling the attacker to retrieve IAM role credentials from the instance metadata service and access S3 buckets containing 100 million customer records. Lesson: cloud security requires understanding service interaction patterns, not just network segmentation.

Security groups are stateful (return traffic automatically allowed), applied per-instance, support allow-only rules. NACLs are stateless (explicit return traffic rules needed), applied per-subnet, support allow and deny rules. Use security groups for instance-level control and NACLs as a second layer of defense at the subnet level.

Without PrivateLink, accessing AWS services (S3, DynamoDB) requires internet routing, exposing traffic to potential interception. PrivateLink creates a private endpoint within your VPC, routing traffic through AWS's internal network. This is essential for compliance (HIPAA, PCI-DSS) and for preventing data exfiltration through public endpoints.

Cloud infrastructure defined in Terraform/CloudFormation should be scanned for security issues before deployment. Tools like Checkov, tfsec, and cfn-nag check for misconfigurations (open security groups, unencrypted storage, public access) in the code, preventing insecure configurations from ever reaching production.

class_schedule.topic: Network Security Capstone: Design and Defense Project
learning_objectives: Synthesize all network security concepts into a comprehensive security architecture
Present and defend a network security design against peer review
Evaluate trade-offs between security, usability, cost, and performance in network design
Demonstrate competence across all course topics through a capstone defense exercise
readings: Review all assigned textbook chapters and NIST publications
NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
Class notes and assignments from weeks 1-14

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session is a capstone integration that brings together every network security concept from the course. You will present your final design project -- a comprehensive network security architecture for a given scenario -- and defend it against peer review and instructor challenges. Real-world network security is not about deploying individual technologies in isolation; it is about building an integrated architecture where firewalls, IDS/IPS, VPNs, NAC, SIEM, email security, DNS security, and cloud controls work together as a coherent defense. Every design decision involves trade-offs. Stricter firewall rules improve security but may break applications or frustrate users. Full packet capture provides maximum visibility but requires enormous storage and raises privacy concerns. SSL inspection enables threat detection in encrypted traffic but breaks end-to-end encryption and requires certificate deployment. Zero trust provides the strongest access control but requires significant infrastructure investment and may impact user experience. The best network security architects understand these trade-offs and make informed decisions that balance security with operational requirements. This session tests your ability to integrate knowledge, communicate design decisions, and respond to challenges -- skills that define effective security practitioners.
key_concepts: Security architecture review: present design, explain each component's role, justify design decisions with threat model references
Attack surface analysis: identify all entry points (internet, wireless, VPN, cloud, physical), assess controls at each point
Defense-in-depth validation: verify that compromise of any single control does not lead to complete system compromise
Compliance mapping: map security controls to regulatory requirements (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR)
Incident response integration: ensure the architecture supports detection, containment, and recovery (SIEM visibility, network isolation capabilities)
Cost-benefit analysis: compare security control costs against risk reduction and compliance requirements
Operational sustainability: can the security team actually manage this architecture with available staff and tools?
Future-proofing: how does the architecture adapt to cloud migration, remote work expansion, IoT proliferation, and post-quantum cryptography?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Present a reference network security architecture for a healthcare organization with 500 employees, 3 offices, cloud workloads in AWS, remote workers, and HIPAA compliance requirements. Walk through each design decision: dual-firewall DMZ for the patient portal, WPA3-Enterprise for wireless, 802.1X for wired, IPSec site-to-site VPN between offices, WireGuard for remote workers, Suricata IDS with ELK SIEM, SPF+DKIM+DMARC for email, DNSSEC with DNS-over-TLS, VPC architecture in AWS with PrivateLink for database access, zero trust proxy for application access, and cloud security posture management. Discuss trade-offs and alternatives for each component.
setup_requirements: Network diagramming tool for architecture presentation
Reference architecture from the final project
Peer review rubric and evaluation criteria
All course notes and reference materials for defense

review.value: A network security architecture is more than the sum of its parts. The firewall logs feed the SIEM. The IDS alerts trigger automated firewall blocks. The NAC enforces device health before granting access. The VPN provides encrypted transport. The zero trust proxy verifies identity per-request. Each component strengthens the others.

Flat networks with no segmentation. Firewalls with allow-any rules. IDS deployed but no one monitors the alerts. VPN with split tunneling enabled. No DNS security. Email without DMARC. Cloud security groups with 0.0.0.0/0. Each of these represents a gap that attackers routinely exploit.

Security controls that frustrate users get bypassed. A VPN that drops frequently leads users to disable it. MFA that requires a hardware token every time leads users to share tokens. The best security architecture is one that users actually use because it does not impede their work.

Network security is not a one-time deployment. New vulnerabilities are discovered daily, new attack techniques emerge, business requirements change, and cloud services evolve. The architecture must include processes for vulnerability management, detection rule updates, incident response exercises, and architecture reviews.

questions.question: Design a network architecture for a small company with a public web server, internal corporate network, and guest Wi-Fi. Specify where you would place firewalls, which zones you would create, and write two specific firewall rules (in pseudocode) that enforce the security policy.
questions.answer: Three zones: DMZ (public web server), Internal (corporate workstations and file server), Guest (Wi-Fi for visitors). Dual-firewall architecture: external firewall between internet and DMZ, internal firewall between DMZ and Internal/Guest. Rules: (1) ALLOW tcp from ANY to DMZ_WEBSERVER:443 -- permits HTTPS traffic to the public web server. (2) DENY all from GUEST_ZONE to INTERNAL_ZONE -- prevents guest devices from accessing corporate resources. Additional rules: ALLOW tcp from DMZ_WEBSERVER to INTERNAL_DB:5432 -- web server can reach its database. DENY all from DMZ to INTERNAL except the database rule above. Implicit deny-all as the last rule on both firewalls.

rubric.key: Correct zone design with DMZ isolation
Two valid firewall rules with justification
Guest isolation properly addressed

questions.question: Write a Snort rule that detects potential SSH brute force attacks: more than 5 failed connection attempts within 60 seconds from the same source IP to any internal host on port 22. Explain each component of the rule.
questions.answer:
alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 22 (msg:"SSH Brute Force Attempt"; flow:to_server,established; content:"SSH-"; detection_filter:track by_src, count 5, seconds 60; sid:1000002; rev:1;)

Components: alert = action (generate alert). tcp = protocol. $EXTERNAL_NET any = source IP range and any port. -> $HOME_NET 22 = destination internal network on SSH port. flow:to_server,established = match traffic going to the server on established connections. content:"SSH-" = match SSH protocol banner. detection_filter:track by_src, count 5, seconds 60 = trigger only after 5 matches from the same source within 60 seconds. sid:1000002 = unique rule ID. rev:1 = rule revision.

rubric.key: Correct Snort rule syntax
Appropriate use of detection_filter for rate-based detection
Clear explanation of each component

questions.question: A company deploys an IDS in passive mode (monitoring a SPAN port) and an IPS inline on the same network segment. The IDS generates 500 alerts per day. After switching the IPS to enforcement mode, legitimate business applications start failing. Explain why this happened and describe the correct deployment process.
questions.answer: The 500 daily IDS alerts likely included many false positives: legitimate traffic that matched overly broad detection rules. In IDS mode, these false positives were merely logged and could be ignored. When the same rules were enforced in IPS mode, they blocked the legitimate traffic (false positive blocking), breaking business applications. The correct deployment process: (1) Deploy in IDS mode for 2-4 weeks. (2) Review every alert category and suppress rules that generate false positives for legitimate traffic. (3) Whitelist known-good traffic patterns that trigger false positives. (4) Gradually move high-confidence rules to IPS mode one category at a time. (5) Monitor application health after each change.
